name: Hotei
description: Hotei, also known as the Laughing Buddha, is a popular figure in Japanese and Chinese folklore. He is often depicted as a jovial, bald man with a large belly and a cheerful smile, symbolizing contentment, happiness, and abundance. While often mistaken for the historical Buddha, Siddhartha Gautama, Hotei is believed to be based on a real-life eccentric Chan Buddhist monk who lived in China during the Later Liang dynasty (907–923 AD). His name means "Cloth Bag" referring to the bag he carries, filled with treasures and candies for children. Hotei is considered a deity of good fortune, prosperity, and guardians of children. He is believed to bring happiness and abundance wherever he goes. Rubbing his belly is said to bring good luck. He is often associated with Zen Buddhism and is seen as an embodiment of enlightenment through simplicity and joy. Hotei's image is frequently found in homes, restaurants, and businesses as a symbol of prosperity and well-being. He is a reminder to appreciate life's simple pleasures and to cultivate a sense of contentment and joy. While not officially part of the Shinto pantheon, Hotei is widely revered in Japan, often included as one of the Seven Lucky Gods (Shichi Fukujin). He represents a blend of Buddhist and folk traditions, symbolizing a lighthearted approach to spirituality and the importance of finding happiness in everyday life.
subtitle: The Laughing Buddha of Happiness

keyMyths.title: The Origin of Hotei
keyMyths.description: Hotei is believed to be based on a real-life eccentric Chinese Chan Buddhist monk who roamed the countryside, bringing joy and small gifts to people. His origins are rooted in the Later Liang dynasty.

keyMyths.title: Hotei's Bag of Treasures
keyMyths.description: Hotei carries a large cloth bag filled with treasures, candies, and rice, which he gives to those in need, symbolizing generosity and abundance. The bag represents a storehouse of good fortune.

keyMyths.title: Hotei as a Lucky God
keyMyths.description: In Japan, Hotei is revered as one of the Seven Lucky Gods, bringing good fortune, prosperity, and happiness to those who worship him. He is often depicted alongside the other gods in celebratory processions.

sources.author: Frederick Starr

worshipPractices.name: Statue veneration
worshipPractices.description: Hotei statues are often placed in homes, businesses, and temples. People rub his belly for good luck, wealth, and happiness. The practice is meant to invoke his blessings and positive energy.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Small offerings of food, coins, and incense are sometimes left at Hotei statues as a sign of respect and to seek his blessings. These offerings are symbolic gestures of gratitude and devotion.

festivals.name: Hotei Festival
festivals.timing: Varies by region, often celebrated around the lunar new year
festivals.description: Festivals dedicated to Hotei involve parades, dances, and special temple ceremonies. People celebrate his jovial spirit and seek his blessings for prosperity and happiness in the coming year.

cultural.modernLegacy: Hotei remains a popular symbol of happiness, good fortune, and contentment in modern East Asian culture. His image is frequently used in art, advertising, and popular culture, representing a positive outlook on life and the pursuit of inner peace.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Hotei's origins are rooted in Chinese Chan Buddhism. He is believed to have been inspired by a real-life eccentric monk named Qiezhiluo, who lived during the Later Liang dynasty (907–923 AD). Qiezhiluo was known for his unconventional behavior, carrying a large cloth sack and wandering from place to place, giving sweets and small gifts to children. He was considered a wandering bodhisattva, a kind of enlightened being. After his death, legends grew around him, and he became a folk hero associated with good fortune and happiness. The name 'Hotei' is the Japanese reading of his Chinese name, Budai, which literally means 'cloth sack.' Over time, Hotei's image and stories spread throughout East Asia, becoming a popular figure in both Chinese and Japanese folklore. He was never considered a historical Buddha, but rather an embodiment of joy and abundance.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: While not a central figure in canonical Buddhist scriptures, Hotei is the subject of numerous folk tales and anecdotes. One common story tells of Hotei wandering through villages, predicting the future and bringing good luck to those he encountered. His large sack was said to contain treasures and necessities, which he would freely distribute to the needy. Another popular story recounts how children would gather around him, drawn to his cheerful demeanor and the treats he offered. Hotei's laughter and carefree spirit were seen as a sign of enlightenment and inner peace. He is often depicted surrounded by children, symbolizing his benevolent nature and his ability to bring joy to others. These stories emphasize his role as a bringer of happiness, prosperity, and protection from misfortune, solidifying his place in popular folklore.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Hotei is not typically worshipped in the same way as traditional deities, but rather venerated as a symbol of good fortune and happiness. Statues of Hotei are commonly found in homes, restaurants, and businesses, particularly in East Asia. People often rub his large belly, believing it will bring them good luck, wealth, and contentment. This act of rubbing the belly is a tactile way of connecting with the positive energy and blessings associated with Hotei. In some Buddhist temples, Hotei is placed among the Seven Lucky Gods (Shichifukujin), further solidifying his status as a bringer of good fortune. While formal rituals are not typically performed in his name, offerings of food and incense may be left at his statues as a sign of respect and gratitude. His image serves as a reminder to cultivate joy, generosity, and a positive outlook on life.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Hotei's legacy extends far beyond religious contexts, influencing art, literature, and popular culture. His image is frequently used in paintings, sculptures, and other forms of art, often depicted with a cheerful smile and a large belly, embodying contentment and abundance. He appears in numerous folk tales and children's stories, reinforcing his role as a benevolent figure who brings happiness and good fortune. In modern times, Hotei's image is used in advertising and marketing to promote products and services associated with luck, prosperity, and well-being. His enduring popularity demonstrates the universal appeal of his message of joy, generosity, and inner peace. He remains a beloved figure, reminding people to embrace a positive outlook on life and to find happiness in simple pleasures.

shortDescription: Hotei is a beloved figure in Japanese and Chinese folklore, embodying happiness, contentment, and abundance. Often called the Laughing Buddha.
